findings.summary: Application-layer module imports from service and core layers unconditionally
evidence: health_checker.py:18-28: unconditional top-level imports from codomyrmex.coding, codomyrmex.performance, codomyrmex.security, codomyrmex.logistics, codomyrmex.environment_setup, codomyrmex.git_operations
health_checker.py:27: imports codomyrmex.performance at module load time — any machine without performance deps installed will fail to import system_discovery.health entirely
health_checker.py:26: imports codomyrmex.logistics.orchestration.project.workflow_dag — orchestrator is a Service Layer dependency pulled into Application Layer health checking code
health_checker.py:28: imports codomyrmex.security.digital.analyze_file_security — security module unconditionally coupled to discovery health checker
system_discovery/__init__.py: does NOT re-export HealthChecker, yet mcp_tools.py:17 does `from codomyrmex.system_discovery import HealthChecker` — this import would fail at runtime
findings.suggestion: Guard heavy imports inside the specific check methods using try/except ImportError. Replace unconditional top-level imports with lazy per-method imports so that importing system_discovery.health never fails due to optional dependency absence.

findings.summary: Two import paths for get_logger used across 678+ modules with no clear rule
evidence: 266 files import via `from codomyrmex.logging_monitoring import get_logger` (public surface)
412 files import via `from codomyrmex.logging_monitoring.core.logger_config import get_logger` (internal path)

agents/editing_loop.py:29-34: uses a try/except ImportError around the logging import with stdlib fallback — uniquely defensive in a way that sibling modules (orchestrator.py, autonomous.py) do not do
system_discovery/core/health_checker.py:22-32: also has its own try/except with setup_logging() side effect — inconsistent with other modules
No declared canonical import path in the module's README or __init__.py
findings.suggestion: Decide on one canonical import path (`from codomyrmex.logging_monitoring import get_logger`) and enforce it via ruff rule or project convention. Remove the try/except ImportError fallbacks in production modules — if logging_monitoring is unavailable the system is broken anyway.

findings.summary: react_step MCP tool returns status=success even when an exception is caught
evidence: agents/core/mcp_tools.py:195-205: the except block of react_step returns `{"status": "success", ...}` with a `note` field containing the exception string — callers see success even on total failure
agents/core/mcp_tools.py:197: `"status": "success"` is hardcoded in the failure path — inconsistent with every other MCP tool in the codebase which return `{"status": "error"}`
All 50+ sibling mcp_tools.py files use `{"status": "error", "message": str(e)}` in their except blocks as a consistent convention
The discrepancy means callers cannot uniformly check `result["status"] == "error"` to detect failures in the agents.core namespace
findings.suggestion: Change the except block in react_step to return `{"status": "error", "message": str(e)}` — consistent with the rest of the MCP tool convention. The fallback planning output can be moved to a degraded-mode path with `"status": "degraded"` if partial results are still useful.

findings.summary: _determine_overall_status returns DEGRADED in both branches of its elif/else
evidence: health_checker.py:182-185: `elif len(result.issues) > 2: return HealthStatus.DEGRADED` and `else: return HealthStatus.DEGRADED` — both branches are identical

The DEGRADED status distinction between 1-2 issues vs. 3+ issues is completely lost — the else branch that was presumably meant to return something lighter (e.g. WARNING or a numeric severity) was never completed
This makes the health checker useless for distinguishing marginal degradation from serious degradation
No test catches this because the function always returns DEGRADED for any non-UNHEALTHY issue set
findings.suggestion: Decide on the intended behaviour: if 1-2 non-critical issues should return a different level (e.g. WARNING or a new MINOR status), add it. If all non-critical issues are DEGRADED regardless of count, remove the elif and collapse to a single else clause.

findings.summary: Two different HealthChecker classes exist in sibling subdirectories with conflicting responsibility
evidence: system_discovery/core/health_checker.py: exports SystemHealthChecker — lightweight git/dep dashboard for the CLI status command
system_discovery/health/health_checker.py: exports HealthChecker — heavyweight multi-module health check framework with Docker, performance, security, logistics dependencies
Both files are named `health_checker.py` in peer subdirectories with no clear ownership boundary
system_discovery/__init__.py exports neither, but mcp_tools.py:17 tries to import HealthChecker from the package — this import fails silently under the except block
system_discovery/core/context.py:12 imports from health/health_checker but core/ and health/ are peer directories — neither is clearly upstream
findings.suggestion: Merge the two health checker files into a single coherent module. Export SystemHealthChecker for the lightweight CLI path and HealthChecker for the full framework path from a single system_discovery/health_checker.py. Add HealthChecker to __init__.py __all__ so mcp_tools.py import succeeds.

findings.summary: Plotter class wraps five chart functions with no added logic or policy
evidence: plotter.py:39-58: every method in Plotter (bar_chart, scatter_plot, histogram, pie_chart) is a one-line delegation to the corresponding module-level function with no pre/post processing
plotter.py:43-46: line_plot sets a figure_size default — the only distinguishing logic in the entire class, and it could trivially be a default argument on create_line_plot itself
plotter.py:61-63: heatmap method calls the module-level create_heatmap which is defined in the same file — the class wrapper adds pure noise
The module-level functions are also re-exported directly in __all__, so callers are never forced to use the class
No caller examined uses Plotter as an interface boundary; the class gains no polymorphism benefit
findings.suggestion: Remove the Plotter class and expose the module-level functions as the primary API. If a stateful plotting context is genuinely needed later, introduce it with real state (e.g. saved theme, output directory). The figure_size default logic can move to create_line_plot's signature.

findings.summary: AgentRequest is defined in two separate modules with different fields and no shared base
evidence: agents/core/base.py:39-57: AgentRequest has fields: prompt, context, capabilities, timeout, metadata, id — the canonical domain model
agents/llm_client.py:21-24: AgentRequest has only: prompt, metadata — a reduced copy with the same class name and no relationship to the base.py version
agents/editing_loop.py:36 and agents/orchestrator.py:43: both import AgentRequest from llm_client — not from core.base
agents/core/mcp_tools.py:53 and :182: imports AgentRequest from core.base within method bodies
ide/antigravity/relay_endpoint.py:32: imports from llm_client — so two separate incompatible AgentRequest types circulate in the same agent workflow
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into the single canonical AgentRequest in agents/core/base.py. Update llm_client.py to import and re-export from there. Migrate all call sites. The simplified llm_client.AgentRequest fields (prompt+metadata only) should be handled by making the core.base fields optional — they already have defaults.

findings.summary: Module docstring appears after import statements instead of at the top of the file
evidence: agents/core/base.py:1-8: six import lines appear before the module docstring on line 9: `"""Core agent interfaces and base classes."""`
system_discovery/health/health_checker.py:1-28: all imports appear first; module docstring appears at line 30-36 after the import block
Python convention (PEP 257) requires the module docstring to be the very first statement in the file, before any imports
All other examined modules (orchestrator.py, editing_loop.py, autonomous.py, mcp_tools.py files) correctly place docstrings first
This is a systematic outlier within the agents/core/ and system_discovery/health/ subdirectories
findings.suggestion: Move the module docstring to line 1 in both files. In base.py, the four blank lines before `logger = get_logger(...)` should also be reduced to one.

findings.summary: Inline comments in health_checker restate what the next line does rather than explaining why
evidence: health_checker.py:39: `# Import logging` directly above `try: logger = get_logger(...)` — imports are self-evident
health_checker.py:219: `# Test logger creation` directly above `test_logger = get_logger(...)` — duplicates the code
health_checker.py:223: `# Test structured logging` directly above `log_with_context(...)` — restates the call
health_checker.py:383: `# Basic check - module import already verified` on a no-body function — explains that nothing happens, not why
health_checker.py:388: identical pattern to 383 — copy-pasted comment for a different empty method
health_checker.py:365: `client.ping()  # Test connection` — the method name `ping()` already communicates this
findings.suggestion: Remove comments that restate the code. Replace with comments only where non-obvious decisions are made (e.g. why tempfile is used, why a specific Docker API call is chosen). The `# Basic check - module import already verified` stubs should either be deleted or have their TODO clarified.

findings.summary: Standard library modules imported inside method bodies instead of at module top-level
evidence: editing_loop.py:404: `import re` inside _parse_plan_edits — re is a stdlib module with negligible import cost
editing_loop.py:499: `import re` again inside _parse_score — second deferred import of the same stdlib module
editing_loop.py:511: `import re` a third time inside _parse_notes — three deferred imports of re in the same class
editing_loop.py:130 (__post_init__): `import os` inside a dataclass __post_init__ — also deferred for a stdlib module
Deferred imports of stdlib modules provide no benefit (they are always available) but add noise and hide dependencies from the module-level import surface
findings.suggestion: Move `import re` and `import os` to the top-level import block. Reserve deferred imports for optional third-party dependencies that may not be installed (as done correctly for codomyrmex.ide.antigravity).

findings.summary: health_check MCP tool imports HealthChecker from a package that does not export it
evidence: mcp_tools.py:17: `from codomyrmex.system_discovery import HealthChecker` — this symbol is not in system_discovery/__init__.py __all__ or defined at package level
system_discovery/__init__.py:47-53: __all__ exports SystemDiscovery, StatusReporter, CapabilityScanner, get_system_context, cli_commands — no HealthChecker
The except block at mcp_tools.py:31 catches the AttributeError and returns an error dict — making this a silent runtime failure rather than a caught import-time error
Calling `health_check()` will always return `{"status": "error", "message": "cannot import name 'HealthChecker'..."}`  unless the package is patched
findings.suggestion: Either export HealthChecker from system_discovery/__init__.py, or change the import in mcp_tools.py to `from codomyrmex.system_discovery.health.health_checker import HealthChecker`. Add an integration test that actually calls the health_check MCP tool to catch this class of import failure.

findings.summary: AntigravityCodeImplementerWrapper is defined as an unnamed inline class inside a function body
evidence: orchestrator.py:220-231: a class `AntigravityCodeImplementerWrapper` is defined inline inside the `_create_llm_client` function body — not at module level

orchestrator.py:221: the class docstring says it 'adapts a base IDE client to the execute_with_session interface' — this is a legitimate adapter, but hiding it inside a conditional branch makes it untestable
orchestrator.py:225: `execute_with_session` on the wrapper has parameters `session=None, session_id=None` that it never uses — misleading signature
The wrapper has no __repr__, no error handling, and cannot be referenced by name externally for testing or isinstance checks
This pattern repeats the core abstraction problem: an adapter that should be a named module-level class is improvised inline each time the condition fires
findings.suggestion: Promote AntigravityCodeImplementerWrapper to a module-level class in orchestrator.py or in a dedicated adapters.py file. Remove unused session/session_id parameters. Add a unit test verifying the adapter's delegation behaviour.

findings.summary: MCP tool import paths are not verified by any integration test, allowing silent runtime failures
evidence: system_discovery/mcp_tools.py health_check tool imports a symbol that does not exist in the package — this would only be caught at call time, not at import time or test collection
agents/core/mcp_tools.py react_step: the except block returns status=success with a phantom response — no test verifies the failure path returns a distinguishable error state
228 occurrences of `return {"status": "error"}` across 51 mcp_tools.py files, but cross-package import validity is only checked through the mcp_bridge scan which logs failures at debug level
No examined test file exercises the full round-trip: mcp_bridge discovery → tool call → verify response shape — test_mcp_proxy_tools.py tests the bridge layer but not concrete tool call outcomes
findings.suggestion: Add a lightweight integration test suite that imports every mcp_tools.py module, calls each tool with minimal valid arguments, and asserts the response always contains a `status` key with value `success` or `error` (never missing). This would catch the HealthChecker import failure and the react_step success-on-failure bug.

findings.summary: system_discovery/health/health_checker.py pulls in six heavy modules unconditionally at import time
evidence: health_checker.py:18: `from codomyrmex.coding.execution.executor import execute_code` — code execution sandbox
health_checker.py:19: `from codomyrmex.coding.static_analysis import analyze_file` — static analysis tools
health_checker.py:26: `from codomyrmex.logistics.orchestration.project.workflow_dag import WorkflowDAG` — orchestration DAG
health_checker.py:27: `from codomyrmex.performance import get_system_metrics, profile_function` — performance monitoring
health_checker.py:28: `from codomyrmex.security.digital import analyze_file_security` — security scanning
Each of these has optional dependencies (Docker, static analysis tools, optional SDKs) — importing health_checker in a minimal environment will fail for any missing transitive dep
findings.suggestion: Move all module-specific imports inside their respective `_check_*` method bodies, guarded by try/except ImportError. The health checker should be importable on any machine regardless of which optional modules are installed.
